evidence: Timeline trailing-end derivation is duplicated in multiple hooks instead of being centralized (`resolveTrailingEffectiveEnd` in `src/tools/travel-between-images/components/Timeline/hooks/useTimelineOrchestrator.ts:155` and near-identical `trailingEffectiveEnd` logic in `src/tools/travel-between-images/components/Timeline/hooks/useTimelineViewportController.ts:67`).

Lightbox state composition uses very wide config and return bags (`UseSharedLightboxStateInput` / `UseSharedLightboxStateReturn` in `src/shared/components/MediaLightbox/hooks/useSharedLightboxState.ts:122` and `:137`), then `ImageLightbox` builds/threads these through several orchestration layers (`src/shared/components/MediaLightbox/ImageLightbox.tsx:200`, `:568`, `:893`).

Timeline view layer and orchestrator both expose broad multi-concern interfaces (`TimelineProps` in `src/tools/travel-between-images/components/Timeline.tsx:25` and `UseTimelineOrchestratorProps` + `UseTimelineOrchestratorReturn` in `src/tools/travel-between-images/components/Timeline/hooks/useTimelineOrchestrator.ts:28` and `:64`), increasing coupling between rendering, drag/drop, selection, endpoint editing, and lightbox integration.

Edge function completion flow remains a long coordinator doing request parsing, auth, rate-limit, storage, generation creation, orchestrator reconciliation, and billing sequencing in one handler (`src/supabase/functions/complete_task/index.ts:98` through `:387`), while similarly complex service-role/user-path branching is repeated in sibling handlers (`src/supabase/functions/claim-next-task/index.ts:61`, `:163`; `src/supabase/functions/generate-upload-url/index.ts:59`, `:74`).

findings.summary: Trailing-end timeline math is duplicated across hooks, creating drift risk.
evidence: `resolveTrailingEffectiveEnd` is implemented in `useTimelineOrchestrator` (`:155-177`), while `useTimelineViewportController` reimplements the same conditional logic and offsets (`:67-83`).
A shared timeline utilities module already exists (`timeline-utils.ts`), but this computation is not sourced from it, so future behavior changes must be applied in multiple places.
findings.suggestion: Extract one shared helper (for example `getTrailingEffectiveEnd`) into `Timeline/utils/timeline-utils.ts`, then have both hooks consume it; add a focused unit test for single-image and multi-image-with/without-trailing-video cases.

findings.summary: Lightbox orchestration relies on oversized state contracts that blur ownership.
evidence: `UseSharedLightboxStateInput` groups six nested concern buckets and many callbacks (`useSharedLightboxState.ts:122-131`), and returns another large multi-section object (`:137-246`).

`ImageLightbox` builds this composite input (`ImageLightbox.tsx:200-276`), then additionally builds separate render/workflow models with very large dependency lists (`:568-892`), indicating orchestration concerns remain spread across layers instead of sharply bounded.
Panel rendering is selected via orchestration conditionals in the same model builder (`ImageLightbox.tsx:462-525`), mixing state integration with UI branching and action wiring.
findings.suggestion: Split the current shared contract into narrower adapters by capability (navigation/variants/shot-actions/edit-panel), and pass only capability-specific interfaces to each panel/composer; enforce boundaries with dedicated types per consumer instead of one umbrella return type.

findings.summary: Timeline container API is over-broad, causing cross-feature coupling.
evidence: `TimelineProps` includes a large mix of concerns: data loading coordination, reorder/drop operations, prompt editing, shot creation, segment navigation, lightbox control, and trailing updater registration (`Timeline.tsx:25-90`).

`UseTimelineOrchestratorProps` and return type mirror similar breadth (`useTimelineOrchestrator.ts:28-153`), making the hook a central god-orchestrator for drag, selection, pending operations, viewport, endpoint, and structure-video interactions.

Because both component and hook expose broad contracts, call sites must understand internal orchestration details (for example wiring lightbox adapter and timeline container with many interdependent callbacks in `Timeline.tsx:236-419`).
findings.suggestion: Introduce feature-scoped sub-adapters (e.g., `timelineInteraction`, `timelineViewport`, `timelineLightboxBridge`) and refactor `Timeline` to compose them, reducing top-level prop count and preventing unrelated feature changes from forcing shared contract churn.

findings.summary: Edge task-completion handlers still centralize too many workflow responsibilities.
evidence: `completeTaskHandler` sequences parse/auth/rate-limit/storage/mutation/orchestrator/billing in one flow (`complete_task/index.ts:98-387`), so lifecycle policy changes touch one large coordinator.
`claim-next-task` duplicates substantial service-role vs PAT path logic, including parallel claim/empty/debug/success branches (`claim-next-task/index.ts:61-163` and `:163-240`).

`generate-upload-url` has similar split branch structure for actor resolution and ownership checks (`generate-upload-url/index.ts:59-93`), despite shared request/runtime helpers existing in `_shared`.
This repeated branching pattern across handlers indicates boundary reuse is partial: transport/runtime concerns are centralized, but business-path orchestration is still repeated per endpoint.
findings.suggestion: Extract shared actor-resolution and task-ownership decision policies into `_shared` workflow helpers and refactor each handler to small declarative steps (validate -> authorize actor -> execute domain command -> map response), keeping endpoint files thin coordinators.
